speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any talk about what was going on in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: The less serious.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Year-- this was about beginning, probably. In '39, '38. Not before. Not before. Absolute no. Because people were coming already from Germany They were coming in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they start coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they start coming in '38, '39. Yes. And my mother was reading Jewish papers. And Friday was the day when she was reading. Everybody was out after dinner. And she was reading the papers. She said, come on, Sonia. I will tell you something. Take a look what there is in Germany, what they're writing. I said, Mommy, you know that if they will torture a Jew, the whole city will cry. That was my answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where will they cry?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you touch one Jew--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: So the whole city-- no matter in Poland or whatever in Europe, the whole city will cry that they're killing Jews. That's what I said.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you mean by that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I said, that the Jewish people are crying, all this. They're crying always. The crying babies. I didn't understand something else. They prepared us-- in the school they were talking to us like that. They were telling us. You see?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: The teachers in our schools. They were talking about the Jewish people sometimes, saying that Poland this-- they said that in '17, they were fighting for Poland, the Polish. And take a look, we find the book. She said, in the history that they Jewish population said that they were fighting together with Poland for the Polish land. And this is not true, the Polish teachers were saying. You see, when you're taking a child-- I was not a child already, even. I went into school, I probably was about 10, 12 years, that's already-- I was listening very well. And they were saying about the Jews, always saying that they did it. This was not true that the Polish people did it by themselves. They were fighting for the land.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believed them. Absolutely I believed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your parents? What did they think?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents said to me like that, you have to listen, but you have to have brains by yourself. But remember one thing, your brothers are not here-- because my two brothers-- one brother was all this, but is now Israel. He was studying already in Wilno. My other brother, Itzchak, was studying in Warsaw, in [INAUDIBLE]. This was a seminar for Jewish teachers, for Hebrew teachers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I had only two brothers. Zalman was a year younger than me, but he was in a private gymnasium. And my little brother, Yankele finished [INAUDIBLE]. There was [NON-ENGLISH], and they hated the Polish. I said, what are doing to you? You cannot see that they want to kill us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, no. I have friends, Polish boys, Polish girls. They were coming to us. We are-- went out for dancing together. They're saying to me, Sonia, wake up. Wake up. And my oldest brother, when he went to Israel, my father gave to him a lot of money. He went with the Aliyah Bet. You could not get. So he had a lot of money with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain to us about the Aliyah Bet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aliyah Bet is words for people that they wanted to go to Israel. They believed in Israel, in Palestine-- that was in Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it legal?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was not legal, but you have to pay a lot of money. And my mother and father took my brother to do all this, to Warsaw. They paid off their money, and they gave it to him. They were Russian moneta. This was Russian money in gold.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about in '38 when the German refugees started arriving? Did you meet any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We met them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived in our houses. Not by ours, but in other houses where they need the money. They took them in. By us, they were coming to eat. We were dancing with them. I had the two friends of mine that they fall in love with them. One is here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you learn from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were learning about how  they live in the big city, in Warsaw-- how they live, enjoy. They were college boys. That was not like us. They were college boys already.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the German refugees?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German refugees, they were telling us horror stories, terrible stories. And we couldn't believe this right away. And they said, yes, you have to believe it. You have to open your eyes. We went out with our life in Poland, from Warsaw, because they wanted to kill us. And then, I didn't believe it that this can be something like that?

speaker: interviewer
text: From Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say from Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Warsaw, yes. They came from Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they come from, originally?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Warsaw. They lived in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said the German Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They came original-- they were Polish, and they lived in Warsaw. But when the Germans begin to come nearer, they were going away from Poland because they knew. But people from Poland that lived near the border, they were telling them terrible stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were Polish Jews who lived in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Warsaw under the German occupation already.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Yeah. Yeah So they were telling us terrible stories. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of Germany invading? When did the Germans come in to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you speaking of 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: '39. '39. First of all, they came the Russians-- came to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us about that? When did the Russians come?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians came in the end of '39. We were very happy that the Polish went away and the Russian came in. And they were singing with us, and playing with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month was that in '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the summer, I think, in July, probably. July and August they came in. Because it was hot. And they were playing outside. And they were dancing outside. And they went around with the Jewish girls. And this was-- they had a beautiful, beautiful time. I was very much sheltered because I was one daughter in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the life change any when they came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little changed, yes. A little changed. My mother said, OK, you can go out with your friends, with your friends. But remember you have to be nine o'clock, eight o'clock home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did life change for your parents in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because they took away all the food from my father's work for all his life. They took away everything. They took away the cheeses. They took away the meat. They took away everything. But my father was such a good man. He had so many workers in these villages. They was bringing him-- they were bringing him butter. They were bringing him flour. They were bringing us everything. We were not hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship of the population with the Russian army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was nice. It was nice. They didn't torch us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did they stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: They stayed from '39, in summer, till the Germans came in '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the Germans come in? What month?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans came in too probably in August. In August. Yes. '39, '40, '41-- two years, they stayed, the Russians. And then, we begin a little to study Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: They stayed from 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: '39 Till '41, in the middle of '41. And the end the '41 was already the first killing of the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when the Germans came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Germans came to Novogrudok?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were gentlemen. When the Germans came to us in Novogrudok, they were gentlemen. They were young, and they were not SS, SD. They were young soldiers. They wanted to flirt. They wanted to talk to us. They wanted to dance with us. They gave us chocolate.

speaker: interviewer
text: They came into the house like they were our friends. And you know what they said to my parents? You have a beautiful daughter. Make papers for her that she's Irish and send her away because when the SD will come, everything will be kaput.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my parents, they're not afraid. I even didn't mention that's what it was telling-- my mother said, don't listen what they're telling you because when we were by the Germans in the first war, they were very good to us. But the Germans was not the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any people believe them at the time and make Aryan papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. But I didn't want it. I could make it because my father was very friendly with the clergyman. They could make me-- one, two three. I didn't understand how can I leave my father and mother and my two brothers and go away in someplace that I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the discussion arise?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the discussion come up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah, because my mother said, you don't have to be afraid of nothing. You went in Poland every year for [NON-ENGLISH]. You went to Poland, to Gdynia, to [INAUDIBLE], to Slonsk, to Krakow, all over with the gymnasium, with the schools. For six weeks, we went around. We went to Zakopane. All over, we went. I never-- in the summer, I went with my mother to Druskienniki. This is on the border from the Litva and Poland-- only for six weeks. And then we went to [INAUDIBLE], where the woman was with my brothers there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were those resort places? Were those summer resorts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, resources, sure. Resources.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things begin to change after the German entry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away. In beginning of December, when they begin to talk about this to the Judenrat. They made the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the Judenrat created?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat-- in November '41. And they begin to ask them-- they need workers to send away to help the army. And they took the young people, and they send them away. They never came back. You know about it. Now, they took ones, they were [? aggravate. ?] This was something, I don't know. And they took 50 from the best people of Novogrudok. They was lawyers, doctors, and they shot them blank [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the marketplace in Novogrudok. Then we knew what is the war. What is the war, meaning from the war. After that-- this was in November. In December, they said that the ghetto has to be built. Everybody from their houses has to go out and we will build you a ghetto. You will be living in the ghetto, the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that notice brought to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was December. We slept already-- my friends was in my room still. I slept with my papa and my mama in her bed. And about two, three o'clock, I was dreaming. And I wake up with crying, terrible. Mommy, I got shot in the head from a German. I waked up my father and mother, and my mother said, OK. We show them this is a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what is it? This is a dream that you have to go away. Something will be. Because she never dreamed, Sonia. And this was the day where they took all of the Jewish people to the graves. So my mother was very good to my father. We had a friend, [? Marvin ?] and he had a brother in Warsaw. He came with the brother from Warsaw with a small girl-- six, seven years old. And he didn't know how to take care of her. So my mother took her to the family. Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they decided that we have to go away this day. We have to go away from Novogrudok. So my mother said, you take the children and go away. You know where to go. And I will take the baby, and I will put him in a very good hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had two horses. We had whatever. We had their silver and everything. And she gave to a neighbor of ours, very rich Polish people. And she said like that, you go into the house in an hour and take everything that you will find in the house. Everything is yours. But remember, you have to watch the baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: The baby is a six-years-old girl. She was a beauty. But remember-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] remember, if you will lose the girl, you will lose your life. And she left the girl, and she ran away. She couldn't find already my father and me and my brother because my father saw already the Germans around the house. So he took us, and  he ran away to the clergyman where he was born, in the same village as my mother was born. And we were there by the clergyman for six days where the potatoes-- in the [NON-ENGLISH] where the potatoes are kept. It was very cold, very cold. But he was very good to us. He was bringing us this hot water and soup and everything. But what he said, Mr. Boldo, you have to go away because the dogs are not letting nobody to sleep. They're watching what is happening here in the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at night, he took me and my father and my brother. And he took us about five kilometers. Then, five kilometers was a lot, a lot. You're not driving a car. You're driving your number 11. And the snow was still here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took us. And he put in to another neighbor-- for my mother's-- that she went to school with him, the clergyman. And we stayed there till after the pogrom. When the ghetto born, he brought us to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the clergyman's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember. But he was a fine, fine man. My father was giving them always-- he was coming to ask-- we had milk in the farm. He was coming and complaining that he has so many people that they need flour, they need something, this and that. So my father said, take your horse and come and take whatever you need it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you mean by the expression 'driving number 11?'

speaker: interviewee
text: 11 is number 11-- our legs, they were calling 11. How are you going? They were saying, with number 11. 11 are the left and the right leg. There were no cars, no horses. You couldn't go off with the horses. They were looking for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the location look like where you were hiding in the place that the clergyman took you to?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a small village, maybe had five or six houses. But one house from the other one was far, far, not like one house near the other one-- very near.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you staying in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in the house. We stayed in the-- where the potatoes. This is up, what they were hearing in the village, what is with the Germans. You understand? They were very much afraid of the Germans, too, because the Germans said, if you will keep a Jew, the whole family is going. The whole family will get killed. But they were very nice. They knew my mother. They knew my father. And they wanted us to stay with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, they went to the city. And they came from the city. They said, Mr. Boldo, the ghetto is already ready. I will take you tomorrow to the ghetto. And that's what happened. He took me and my father, my brother to the ghetto. And the ghetto was full with people. There were no place for us. So Lea's mother and father-- Lea, that you had the interview with her. They had a house, and there were the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lea who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lea [INAUDIBLE] Frydberg. Her mother her father, they took us in. And they gave us a small place where we were sleeping on the floor. They gave us a small piece. We could already sleep there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by 'they made a ghetto?' What did they do? How did they make a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took it out, all the Jewish people from the houses from the whole city. And the put them in a small place they called ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a fence around it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they made a fence. Sure, they made a fence with this. And the police, Polish police, and Ukrainian police, and German police were surrounding us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a fence did they make?

speaker: interviewee
text: The fence, a big fence with the [NON-ENGLISH], iron. You couldn't go out. No. You couldn't go in. You couldn't go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Wire, sure. You couldn't go out. And they had big dogs. Big dogs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was guarding it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was guarding?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was watching--

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: --the ghetto that you don't escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I said to you. The Polish police, the Ukrainian police, and the Germans. That's it. The worst of all of them, they were the Ukrainian. The Ukrainian were the-- worse from Hitler's.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many families stayed in the apartment of your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't count them, but this was-- you know what? Like you see the bees-- bees with the making honey. So the whole house was a very small house. But in one room, maybe it was about 20 people. 20 people, for sure. And one lived with the other quietly. And in the morning, they went to work, everybody. They went out from the ghetto. They took them to the city because the city was ruined by the bombs. So they clean up the city. And they were working there. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you working?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents working?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many members of your family came with you to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: How many? My brother was in the police. Yankele was in the police, and he was inside the ghetto. And I was working in the hospital from the ghetto. They put me in there. I should not go out to the Germans, and they should hit me over the head because they were-- the people, when they went out to the city, they were killing them. They were hitting them-- they should work faster, faster, faster.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father, he knew to whom to give whatever he gave. And they put me in the hospital. And I have the people that they were working with me-- I have here Sonia Orzman. You saw them on the Novogrudok. These are my friends from school. We finished together. We made the exams to Rostov for dentistry. Only me and her got accepted to Rostov. We made the exams. But the war came in two days. Everything was finished. So that's not what you can say something. And this is not true of this, is it? Were your parents with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Always with me. Always with me. My father did many things for the Jewish people in the ghetto. He was very friendly with the head of the police, the Polish police. He was, all life, he had a family from four children. And he didn't have-- never enough, so my father was always giving him food for the family. He gave to him shoes for the family, gave it to him four coats for the family. Everything. But when I needed him, he did everything for me, for my father. My father took it out, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a wife and two children an hour before they took him to the [INAUDIBLE] to kill them. Because she said that she is something-- a family with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father went. He risked his life, and he went to the police. And he said, the police was [INAUDIBLE], the head of the police of Novogrudok.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, [INAUDIBLE], I will do everything for you. He said, you have to take out the lady with the two daughters. And he took her out before an hour to finish the whole ghetto. Now, my husband's two brothers were in there, in the police for dead. We didn't know because I didn't know my brother. I didn't know my husband. I didn't know the brothers. But his mother, she was coming to the ghetto, asks if somebody can do something for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were some people, they were asking her $100 in gold. My husband was telling me about this later. And they didn't have the gold. So they went where all the Jews went.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this time, my father took it out, the woman and the two children. And they came to the United States. And they're writing now a book. [INAUDIBLE] writing a book about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did people get food in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, a Jew is a big [NON-ENGLISH], a Jew. He has always a watch, a ring, a pair of shoes, a pair of boots. And they were changing. When they were on the market and working, the paisanos, they were coming and said, I need a watch. I will give you the food. So they gave to him the watch, the paisano, and he will bring them bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the peasants?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the peasants. That's all. That's the only way. They couldn't buy it. There were [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no food in places to buy. They were bringing them for the morning, for a ring, for boots, for everything. And that's what. And this was a hunger, too, in the ghetto. A lot of hunger. But they were given a piece of bread a day because people, they went to work. So the Germans was giving them a piece of bread. I don't know, but a small piece of bread. And on this bread, they were living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the administrators of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What power did they have?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had power of death. When the Germans tell them that we need, today, 50 people to go to work, they knew right away that is for dead, but they have to give it to them. They gave it to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: They knew it was for what?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have to give it to them, the 50 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: For work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They said for work, but they took them, and they were killing them. Later, we knew about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship of the population with the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish population? They hate them. They hate them because they took away their families. Families, they took away. You take away the head of the family, so the whole family is going down. And the Judenrat didn't have nothing to do with that. They told them you have to give me 50, 100 people. See, my friends, Daniel [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE], we studied together in gymnasium. This was only 3% Jewish. They were the head of the Judenrat because I was studying French. They were studying German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were in the Judenrat. And they knew everything, but they couldn't even take care of their parents. How could they take care of somebody else's parents? How long were you in that ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this ghetto, I was-- this was in December 1941 till April '42. April 15, '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did people deal with illness? Was there a hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had doctors. We had a doctor. Yeah. But we didn't have nothing for the sick, to give. We didn't have nothing. We didn't have medicine. They were dying, and that's it. Listen, better die than to be killed like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any communal life? Was anyone teaching children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You see, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was a war, a terrible war. A war of survival. If you could survive yourself, you didn't give a hoot for nobody else. Mothers and fathers was killing the children because they were in some place in the [INAUDIBLE], and the child was hungry. And they were crying. So they killed them by themselves in order not to-- they should not find out that people are here.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to break now.
